---
# ============================================================
# LECTURE TEMPLATE & SLIDE WRITING GUIDELINES
# Copy this file when starting a new lecture, then delete the
# "SLIDE WRITING GUIDELINES" section before writing content.
# Filename convention: NN-short-slug.qmd  (e.g., 02a-history.qmd)
# Files prefixed with "_" are skipped by Quarto, so this file
# itself is not rendered.
# ============================================================
title: "Lecture N — [Lecture Title]"
subtitle: "Part A: [Subtitle] · Part B: [Subtitle]"
author: "Brendan Shea, PhD"
date: "An Introduction to Bioethics."
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
  revealjs:
    output-file: NN-slug-slides.html
---

# SLIDE WRITING GUIDELINES {.section-divider}

*Delete this whole section when you copy the template. It is the agreed standard for every lecture, derived from review of L1–L3.*

## Audience and Level

- The audience is mostly **nursing students**, plus PSEO (high-schoolers), gen-ed, and some pre-meds. Write for a **capable 12th grader**.
- Anything a 12th grader wouldn't know (an RCT, germline vs. somatic, brain death, a legal standard) gets a tight plain-language explanation the first time — usually in a `.context-box`.
- **Show, don't just name.** Don't write "Willowbrook exposed institutional failure"; tell what concretely happened to real, named people. Conclusions land only after the story.
- Distressing history (Nazi experiments, Tuskegee, sterilization): **concrete but restrained** — name what was done plainly, no graphic detail.

## Slide Density (hard rule)

Every reveal.js slide must fit **one 1050×700 screen** with no scrolling. Prefer splitting over cramming. A content slide carries **about five sentences' worth of material**, broken up as:

- **5 bullets, no header sentence**, OR
- **4 bullets with one header sentence above**, OR
- **3 bullets with a header sentence above and a closing line below**, OR
- a table / quote-card / two-column / context-box that carries equivalent density.

Prefer **full sentences** to fragments. Fragment "lecture cues" ("Not a checklist", "Free meals encouraged participation") are banned — a student must be able to **study from the slide alone**.

A "thought question + reveal" is **two slides**, never one slide with a fragment.

## Study-Worthiness

- Each content slide must teach on its own: real **names, dates, numbers, mechanisms, and outcomes**, not gestures.
- Cut navigational filler ("the next slides will trace…", "as mentioned earlier"). It wastes the ~5-sentence budget.
- If a slide is only a slogan, it is not done.

## Layout Variety

Monotony is a defect. **Never run more than two plain bullet-list slides in a row.** Rotate among these shapes:

| Shape | Use for |
|---|---|
| Prose (5 full sentences) | Narrative, argument, synthesis |
| Two-column **text + image** | Any slide with a photo — the image is a *column*, never dumped under bullets |
| Contrast table | "myth vs. fact", "told vs. true", "I vs. II", competing standards |
| Comparison table | Parallel concepts collapsed into one study table (e.g., Belmont principles) |
| Quote-card | Primary-source quotation (see Quotes) |
| `.context-box` | A mini-case or a concept a 12th grader needs |
| Timeline / flow `{dot}` | Sequences and decision procedures |
| Recurring "scoreboard" table | An intentional running motif — repetition here is *good* |

Collapse repetitive parallel runs (three near-identical principle slides → one rich table). Consolidating and *then* enriching beats leaving thin parallel slides.

## Discussion Units (the 3-slide pattern)

Each major theme gets a discussion **unit**, aiming for ~10 minutes:

1. **`## Thought Question`** — a concrete scenario with **2–3 stacked sub-questions** (put the student in a role).
2. **`## Make It Contemporary`** — a **documented present-day analogue** (real law, case, study, or practice).
3. **`## After You've Discussed`** — a `.callout-note` debrief that introduces a **new distinction the body never stated** (e.g., coercion vs. undue inducement vs. exploitation). It must *advance* the analysis, never merely restate the lecture.

## Quotes

- A stand-alone quote slide uses a **longer, verbatim** passage, not a clipped phrase.
- **Verbatim accuracy is non-negotiable.** Verify against a primary or authoritative source; reproduce punctuation exactly; mark editorial changes with `[brackets]` and cuts with an ellipsis.
- Attribute fully, including **translator/edition** for translated works (e.g., Kant, Abbott trans.).

## Cross-References and Metacommentary

- **Never use `L#` codes** ("L7", "see L2") — students don't know them and numbering changes.
- **No inter-lecture metacommentary** in slides ("as we saw in Lecture 2", "Part B will not re-tell this"). Use plain phrasing: *"later, when we turn to end-of-life care."*
- Treat earlier lectures as **prior knowledge**: recap in one line, then apply — don't re-teach. Minimize overlap between lectures by design.

## Citations

- Use real `refs.bib` keys only. **Never invent a key.** If a source should be cited, add a correct entry to `refs.bib` first.
- Contemporary facts (recent laws, cases, figures) may be stated in prose **without** a citation, consistently — but get them factually right.

## Banned Patterns

- "Read for next class" / reading-assignment slides.
- Slides whose whole body is 3–4 terse fragments.
- Long parallel runs of "X is not Y" / one-word bullets.
- Re-narrating a case already told in full in an earlier lecture.

## Recurring Devices (encouraged)

- A **running spine** (a scoreboard table that accumulates; a single patient followed across the lecture) gives the deck momentum and is worth building deliberately.
- Reusing the *same* motif slide repeatedly is intentional repetition, not monotony — it is exempt from the layout-rotation rule.

# Part A — [Subtitle] {.section-divider}

## Opening: A Case to Sit With

Open with a concrete scenario that frames the lecture's central tension — usually a `.thought-question` (the hook, not the lesson), followed by a short `.callout-note` "After You've Discussed". Make students feel the problem before they get vocabulary.

## What We're Doing Today

::: {.learning-outcomes}
By the end of Part A, you will be able to:

- [Outcome: action verb + specific content; tell what *happened* in named cases, not just "discuss themes"]
- [Outcome]
- [Outcome]
:::

## [Section Title]

Body in **full sentences**. Introduce a [key term]{.key-term} the first time it appears. Keep to the density rule above.

In-text citations use Pandoc/Chicago author-date: @beauchamp2019 argued X; a parenthetical looks like this [@emanuel1992]; multiple at once [@beauchamp2019; @faden1986].

::: {.context-box}
**[Concept a 12th grader may not know]**

Two or three tight sentences of plain-language background. Scaffolding only.
:::

## [Section Title] — varied layout

Rotate the shape (table / two-column+image / quote-card / `dot`). For a diagram, prefer Graphviz `{dot}` or Mermaid (editable) over a static picture:

```{dot}
digraph G {
  rankdir=LR;
  node [shape=box, style=rounded];
  "Refuses treatment" -> "Has capacity?";
  "Has capacity?" -> "Honor refusal" [label="yes"];
  "Has capacity?" -> "Surrogate decides" [label="no"];
}
```

Photographs are columns, with attribution:

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
Four full sentences of substance here.
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
![](../images/example.jpg){width="90%" fig-alt="describe"}

::: {.attribution}
Source. Public domain. [@key]
:::
:::
::::

## Thought Question

::: {.thought-question}
A concrete scenario in which the student has a role, with 2–3 stacked sub-questions, no obvious right answer.
:::

## Make It Contemporary

::: {.thought-question}
A documented present-day analogue (real law/case/study) that re-poses the same tension.
:::

## After You've Discussed

::: {.callout-note}
A debrief that introduces a **new distinction** — never a restatement of the slides above.
:::

## Mini-Recap

::: {.recap}
Two or three sentences synthesizing Part A and bridging into Part B.
:::

# Part B — [Subtitle] {.section-divider}

## Where We Left Off

One short paragraph reorienting a student who watched Part A separately. Online students see A then B back-to-back, so don't over-repeat.

## What We're Doing in Part B

::: {.learning-outcomes}
By the end of Part B, you will be able to:

- [Outcome]
- [Outcome]
:::

## [Section Title]

Build on Part A as prior knowledge (recap in a line, then apply). Same density and layout-variety rules. Run at least one full discussion unit.

## [Application Section]

Work a brief illustrative case in the text, or point to the paired case study **by topic name, not lecture number**:

> **Paired case:** [Case N — Title](cases/NN-slug.qmd)

## Wrap-Up

::: {.recap}
Three or four sentences summarizing the whole arc — the question, the main moves, the unresolved tension. No `L#`.
:::

## Key Terms

- **[Term]** — short gloss
- **[Term]** — short gloss

## Further Reading

Accessible follow-up sources (distinct from works cited).

- Author. *Title*. Publisher, Year.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
